error.signature: Cloudflare Worker exceeds CPU time limit even though wall clock time is well under 30s

verdict.summary: Cloudflare Workers free tier has 10ms CPU time limit (not wall clock time). A request waiting 5s for a fetch() uses ~0ms CPU. But JSON.parse() on a large payload or crypto operations quickly exceed 10ms CPU. Paid plan gets 30s wall clock.

dead_ends.action: Assume 10ms means the Worker must complete in 10 milliseconds wall clock time
dead_ends.why_fails: CPU time ≠ wall clock time. I/O wait (fetch, KV reads) doesn't count. A Worker can run for 30s wall clock if most time is I/O, but 10ms of pure computation is the limit.

dead_ends.action: Optimize fetch calls to reduce total execution time
dead_ends.why_fails: fetch() is I/O and doesn't count toward CPU time. The bottleneck is usually JSON parsing, string manipulation, or crypto operations.

workarounds.action: Move CPU-intensive work to a backend service, use Worker only for routing/transformation
workarounds.how: Worker fetches from origin/backend, does minimal transformation, returns result. Heavy computation stays in backend.

workarounds.action: Upgrade to Workers Paid ($5/month) for 30s wall clock / 30ms CPU per request
workarounds.how: Paid Workers: 30s wall clock for HTTP, 15min for Cron Triggers. Much more generous CPU limits.

workarounds.action: Use streaming responses to avoid large JSON parse operations
workarounds.how: Use HTMLRewriter or streaming JSON parser instead of loading entire response into memory
